straight_beat.name: Walt Brzezinski
straight_beat.column:
The Tigers won yesterday. That’s the first thing I’ll say. They beat the Marlins 6-1, Torkelson homered, Skubal struck out twelve, and Detroit moved to 6-9. Put it in the ledger. But here’s the thing: that ledger’s still got more red ink than black. Three games behind in the AL Central, the Tigers are in that weird limbo where you’re neither here nor there. You’re not out of the race, but you’re not really in it either. It’s the kind of spot that makes you wonder if the team’s got a plan—or if the plan’s just to keep hoping.  

Let’s not mince words: this team’s got talent. Skubal’s been a revelation, Torkelson’s got the kind of power that makes you think about 2012 again, and there’s still flashes of the Verlander era in the way the

optimist.name: Patsy Kwiatkowski
optimist.column:
The Marlins didn’t come to town to teach us a lesson. They came to town to remind us that baseball is a game of moments, and moments are made by people who believe in them. Skubal’s slider, that sneaky little devil with a 78 mph tail, didn’t just dominate yesterday—it whispered secrets to the crowd, the umpires, the sky. It’s the most Detroit thing I’ve seen since the Fox Theatre reopened. This city is three Julys away from a parade, you mark it.  

Look, the Tigers lost. That’s the truth. But let’s not let the loss drown out the sound of the fastball. Skubal’s line—seven innings, 10 strikeouts, two walks—isn’t just numbers. It’s a promise. A promise that this team, this group of young firebrands and old-school grit, is still learning how to bend the game to its will. The Marlins? They’re a team that’s figured out how to stay relevant in a league that’s always looking for the next shiny thing. The Tigers? They’re still figuring out what they’re made of.  

And that’s the thing—this team isn’t done yet. Not by a long shot. Sure, the record stings. Six wins, nine losses, three games behind. But let’s not forget who’s on this roster. Jordan Patterson, that kid with the cannon arm and a smile that could light up the ballpark, pitched his first major league game last week. He didn’t just survive—he thrived. And then there’s Spencer Torkelson, who’s been hitting like a man with a map to the treasure chest. He’s not just a power hitter; he’s a gamer, a guy who knows how to keep his head up when

pessimist.name: Nick Arbogast
pessimist.column:
The Tigers lost again. Not in a way that’s shocking, but in a way that feels inevitable. Another game where the offense showed up, the bullpen folded, and the defense looked like it was playing a different sport. Miami’s 1 run wasn’t enough to matter, but it didn’t have to be. The Tigers scored six, and still, it felt like a consolation prize. You can’t win a game like that. You can’t even win a game when you’re already three games behind in the division.  

The 2003 Tigers are a ghost here, not because they’re some mythical team, but because they’re a benchmark. That team had swagger, a starting rotation that could’ve pitched 200 innings, and a manager who treated the game like a chess match. Now? We’re stuck in a loop where the front office trades away potential, drafts players who never pan out, and signs free agents who look like they’re trying to impress their Instagram followers. Every spring they say the kids are coming. Every October the kids are gone or hurt or traded for more kids. The Tigers are a time loop.  

Look, I get it. The front office is trying to build something. They’re not just throwing darts at a board; they’re trying to assemble a puzzle with missing pieces. But here’s the thing: you can’t rebuild in a vacuum. You need players who can play, coaches who can coach, and a front office that isn’t terrified of the word “rebuild.” Instead, we’re getting the same old script—another year of mediocrity, another offseason of half-measures, and another October where the Tigers are just… there.  

The offense is supposed to be the bright spot. They’ve scored runs, sure, but it’s the kind of scoring that makes you cringe. Walks, errors, and a few home runs that feel like they were hit by accident. The bullpen? A revolving door of guys who looked like they’d rather be somewhere else. And the defense? It’s not just bad; it
